title: Quick Ratio Math for SaaS: Why Solo Founders Should Care
meta_description: The quick ratio tells you if your SaaS is actually growing or just treading water. Here's the math, why it matters, and how to track it without a finance team.
h1: The Quick Ratio Math Every Solo SaaS Founder Should Memorize
body_markdown:
A founder I know hit $18k MRR in March 2024 and posted a celebration tweet. Six months later he was at $19k MRR and panicking, because in those six months he'd added $42k of new MRR and lost $41k to churn and downgrades. He was running on a treadmill at full sprint and the dashboard kept saying he was winning.

That's what the quick ratio catches. And it's the single number I wish someone had drilled into my head when I was doing $4k MRR and feeling clever.

Let me walk you through the actual math, why solo founders specifically tend to ignore it, and what number you should be aiming for before you start scaling anything.

## The math, no hand-waving

Quick ratio for SaaS was popularized by Mamoon Hamid back when he was at Social Capital — the original framing showed up in his investor decks around 2015 and got picked up everywhere after. The formula is dead simple:

**Quick Ratio = (New MRR + Expansion MRR) / (Churned MRR + Contraction MRR)**

That's it. Growth divided by shrinkage. If you added $5,000 in new and expansion MRR last month, and you lost $1,000 to churn and downgrades, your quick ratio is 5.

A few definitions so we're not arguing about terms:

- **New MRR**: customers who didn't exist last month. First-time subscriptions.
- **Expansion MRR**: existing customers who paid you more this month than last month. Upgrades, seat additions, plan bumps.
- **Churned MRR**: customers who fully canceled. Their contribution went from $X to $0.
- **Contraction MRR**: existing customers who paid you less. Downgrades, removed seats, applied discounts they didn't have before.

Notice what's not in there. Total MRR. Revenue. ARR. None of that matters for the quick ratio. The whole point is to ignore the size and look at the velocity.

And that's why it's so useful for someone doing $8k MRR — you can have a great quick ratio at $8k MRR and a terrible one at $800k MRR, and which company would you rather own in two years?

### Why the threshold is 4

Social Capital's original benchmark was a quick ratio of 4 or higher for a "best in class" SaaS company. The logic: for every $1 you lose to churn, you should be adding $4 in new and expansion. That gives you a comfortable buffer for the inevitable bad months without your growth curve turning into a flat line.

Below 1, you're shrinking. Period. Doesn't matter what your top-line MRR says this month — the leak is bigger than the inflow and the math will catch up to you.

Between 1 and 2, you're "growing" but barely. Most of your new business is replacing churned customers. This is the most demoralizing place to be because you're working incredibly hard and the dashboard barely moves.

Between 2 and 4, you're in normal-company territory. Healthy, but not exciting. This is where most B2B SaaS lives.

Above 4, you have something genuinely working. Above 8, you should probably be raising money or hiring or both.

## Why solo founders specifically ignore this

I got this wrong for almost a year. From mid-2022 to early 2023 I was watching MRR go up and feeling great, completely ignoring that my churn was creeping from 3% to 6% to 9%. New signups were masking it. The day I actually computed my quick ratio and got a 1.4 was the day I stopped building features for a month and rewrote my onboarding.

Here's why this happens to solo founders more than to companies with a finance person:

Stripe's dashboard doesn't show it. Open Stripe right now. Go to Billing. You'll see MRR, you'll see active subscribers, you'll see a churn number if you squint. You will not see your quick ratio. ProfitWell (now Paddle Metrics) will show it if you connect it, Baremetrics shows it on their $129/mo Startup plan, ChartMogul shows it — but if you're just eyeballing Stripe directly, you'll never compute this number unless you sit down and do it manually.

And nobody sits down to do it manually. Because manually means exporting CSVs and writing a spreadsheet and remembering to do it every month. Which leads to the second reason.

Solo founders are time-poor in a specific way. You have time to build features. You have time to answer support. You don't have time for the kind of slow, reflective monthly review where you'd notice that your contraction MRR has tripled. The work that would tell you to *stop* doing what you're doing is exactly the work that gets skipped.

Third — and this one's psychological — the quick ratio can be brutal. If you've been telling yourself a story about how things are working, opening a spreadsheet that says "actually you're at 1.6" feels bad. So you don't open it. (I did this. Multiple times. The spreadsheet was open in another tab for three weeks before I looked at it.)

## A worked example with actual numbers

Let's say you're at $12,000 MRR going into October. During October:

- 8 new customers sign up at an average of $79/mo → **$632 new MRR**
- 3 existing customers upgrade plans, adding $40, $99, and $40 → **$179 expansion MRR**
- 2 customers cancel — one was on $79, one was on $149 → **$228 churned MRR**
- 1 customer downgrades from $149 to $79 → **$70 contraction MRR**

Growth side: $632 + $179 = $811
Shrinkage side: $228 + $70 = $298
Quick ratio: 811 / 298 = **2.72**

Your MRR went up by $513 this month. Looks fine on the chart. But your quick ratio is 2.72, which is okay but not great, and the trend matters more than the number. If last month it was 3.8 and this month it's 2.72, something is changing — either churn is accelerating or new growth is slowing or both, and you need to know which.

This is the conversation the quick ratio forces you to have with yourself.

## What to actually do with the number

A quick ratio in isolation is just trivia. It becomes useful when you track it monthly and watch the trend, and when you decompose it.

Because here's the thing: a quick ratio of 3 from $9k new + $3k churn is a totally different company than a quick ratio of 3 from $900 new + $300 churn. Same ratio, wildly different situations. The first company has a churn problem hiding behind aggressive acquisition. The second company is barely a business yet.

So when you compute it, also look at the absolute numbers underneath. Ask:

1. Is the numerator (new + expansion) growing month over month, or am I just maintaining?
2. Is the denominator (churn + contraction) shrinking, growing, or stable as a percentage of total MRR?
3. Is more of my growth coming from expansion than from new? (If yes, that's usually great — expansion is cheaper than acquisition.)
4. What's my expansion-to-new ratio specifically? Companies like Slack and Notion famously had expansion MRR exceeding new MRR at certain stages, which is the holy grail.

That last point is worth sitting with. If you can get one existing customer to pay you $50 more, that costs you basically nothing — no ad spend, no sales call, no onboarding. If you have to acquire a new $50/mo customer, you're paying CAC, you're paying support cost, you're betting they'll stick around long enough to be profitable. Expansion MRR is the cheat code, and the quick ratio is one of the few metrics that gives it equal weight to new business.

> The quick ratio doesn't tell you what to do. It tells you whether what you're already doing is working.

That's the framing I've come to. It's diagnostic, not prescriptive. If your quick ratio drops from 3.5 to 1.8 over three months, the number doesn't tell you to fix onboarding or fix pricing or fix your ICP. It just tells you something is broken and you have maybe two quarters before it shows up in your top-line MRR in a way that's hard to recover from.

## The boring practical part

You can compute this in a spreadsheet. Export your Stripe data, categorize each MRR change as new/expansion/churn/contraction, sum them up, divide. Takes about 90 minutes the first time and 20 minutes every month after that if you build the template right.

Or you use something that does it for you. Baremetrics at $129/mo is probably overkill if you're under $20k MRR. ChartMogul has a free tier up to $10k MRR that's genuinely useful. We built [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) specifically because most of the founders we talked to were either paying $129+ for tools designed for Series A companies or doing nothing — and "nothing" is the option that quietly kills businesses doing $5-30k MRR.

Whatever you use, the action item is the same: compute your quick ratio for the last three months. Right now, before you close this tab. If it's above 4, congratulations, keep doing what you're doing. If it's between 2 and 4, you're fine but pay attention to the trend. If it's below 2, stop building features this week and go figure out where the leak is.

The founder I mentioned at the start eventually got his quick ratio back to 3.1 by killing his cheapest plan and rewriting his cancellation flow. Took him four months. He told me later the worst part wasn't the work — it was realizing he'd spent the previous year optimizing for a number that was lying to him.
